repository_intake_record.name: spring-authorization-server
repository_intake_record.selection_reason: Not in WP2 seed list. Added under 'security infrastructure projects' extension category. Spring Authorization Server is the reference OAuth2/OIDC authorization server for the Spring ecosystem. High-density cryptographic patterns: JWT signing (RSA/EC), PKCE (SHA-256), password encoding (BCrypt), mTLS (X.509), JWK Set management, opaque token storage. Adds: asymmetric key generation, JOSE/JWKS patterns, PKCE code challenge patterns — all absent from apache/shiro batch. High language diversity value (Java).

evidence_records.explanation: SecurityConfig.generateRsaKey() creates KeyPairGenerator.getInstance('RSA'), keySize=2048, returns RSAKey. jwkSource() builds ImmutableJWKSet with generated RSA key. Full method body visible.
JwtGenerator.generate() constructs JwsHeader with JwsAlgorithm from TokenSettings.signingAlgorithm(). Delegates to NimbusJwtEncoder.encode(). Algorithm not hardcoded here — flows from TokenSettings.

CodeVerifierAuthenticator.verify() branches on code_challenge_method. S256 branch: MessageDigest.getInstance('SHA-256') applied to code_verifier bytes. Plain branch: direct string comparison. SHA-256 confirmed for S256.

RegisteredClient stores clientSecret as plain String field. PasswordEncoder is applied externally by InMemoryRegisteredClientRepository or JdbcRegisteredClientRepository. Algorithm configuration-driven — not hardcoded here.
NimbusJwkSetEndpointFilter.doFilterInternal() calls jwkSource.get(jwkSelector, context) and serializes result via JWKSet.toString(). Serves public JWK Set. Key type determined by injected JWKSource.
OAuth2RefreshTokenGenerator.generate() uses Base64.getUrlEncoder().encodeToString(DEFAULT_TOKEN_BYTES_LENGTH). Must confirm entropy source — likely SecureRandom or UUID. Full body included.
TokenSettings.signingAlgorithm() returns SignatureAlgorithm. Default set in withDefaults(). Access token format via accessTokenFormat(). Algorithm selection chain confirmed.
X509CertificateGeneratorApplication generates self-signed X.509 certificate. KeyPairGenerator, certificate builder, and algorithm parameters visible in full method body.

name: Build and Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - '**'
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 10 * * *' # Once per day at 10am UTC
  workflow_dispatch:

env:
  DEVELOCITY_ACCESS_KEY: ${{ secrets.GRADLE_ENTERPRISE_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}

permissions:
  contents: read

jobs:
  build:
    name: Build
    uses: spring-io/spring-security-release-tools/.github/workflows/build.yml@v1
    strategy:
      matrix:
        os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
        jdk: [17]
    with:
      runs-on: ${{ matrix.os }}
      java-version: ${{ matrix.jdk }}
      distribution: temurin
    secrets: inherit
  test:
    name: Test Against Snapshots
    uses: spring-io/spring-security-release-tools/.github/workflows/test.yml@v1
    with:
      test-args: --refresh-dependencies --stacktrace -PforceMavenRepositories=snapshot -PspringFrameworkVersion=7.+ -PspringSecurityVersion=7.+
    secrets: inherit
  deploy-artifacts:
    name: Deploy Artifacts
    needs: [build, test]
    uses: spring-io/spring-security-release-tools/.github/workflows/deploy-artifacts.yml@v1
    with:
      should-deploy-artifacts: ${{ needs.build.outputs.should-deploy-artifacts }}
      default-publish-milestones-central: true
    secrets: inherit
  deploy-docs:
    name: Deploy Docs
    needs: [build, test]
    uses: spring-io/spring-security-release-tools/.github/workflows/deploy-docs.yml@v1
    with:
      should-deploy-docs: ${{ needs.build.outputs.should-deploy-artifacts }}
    secrets: inherit
  perform-release:
    name: Perform Release
    needs: [deploy-artifacts, deploy-docs]
    uses: spring-io/spring-security-release-tools/.github/workflows/perform-release.yml@v1
    with:
      should-perform-release: ${{ needs.deploy-artifacts.outputs.artifacts-deployed }}
      project-version: ${{ needs.deploy-artifacts.outputs.project-version }}
      milestone-repo-url: https://repo1.maven.org/maven2
      release-repo-url: https://repo1.maven.org/maven2
      artifact-path: org/springframework/security/spring-security-oauth2-authorization-server
      slack-announcing-id: spring-authorization-server-announcing
    secrets: inherit

adjudication_records.adjudicator_notes: Human review: RS256 (RSA PKCS#1v1.5 + SHA-256) confirmed hardcoded in JwtGenerator:99. Evidence chain complete (method_body + dependency_declaration). Not a critical weakness but worth flagging for algorithm agility review.

label_corrections.labels.security_concern_note: PKCS1v15 padding is legacy; PS256 (RSA-PSS) preferred per NIST SP 800-131A

adjudication_records.human_reasoning: RS256 uses PKCS#1 v1.5 signature padding, which is deterministic and has known theoretical weaknesses compared to probabilistic PSS. Spring Security does support PS256 via SignatureAlgorithm.PS256. This is a real, actionable recommendation. Not a showstopper but should be in the CBOM with a migration note.

adjudication_records.adjudicator_notes: Human review: JWK Set endpoint serving confirmed. Evidence direct (method_body). Minor correction: cryptographic_operation='key_exchange' is imprecise. The endpoint publishes public keys (JWK Set), not performs a key exchange protocol. More accurate label: 'encode' (serializing JWK Set to JSON) or keep key_exchange with clarification. Accepting with label correction to 'encode'.

label_corrections.labels.cryptographic_operation_note: JWK Set endpoint encodes/publishes public keys — not a Diffie-Hellman-style key exchange

adjudication_records.human_reasoning: JWK Set endpoint serves public keys to token verifiers. This is key publishing, not an interactive key agreement protocol. 'encode' captures that the JWK Set is serialized (JSON encoded) and served. The quantum relevance is correct — the published keys are RSA/EC which are quantum-vulnerable.

adjudication_records.adjudicator_notes: Human review: TokenSettings.withDefaults() RS256 default confirmed from field_or_constant evidence. Finding is complementary to HAR-002 and HAR-025 (different analysis tasks). No duplication issue — separate tasks with different entry points. quantum_vulnerable_public_key accurate. No active weakness at current key size. Accepted.
adjudication_records.human_reasoning: TokenSettings.withDefaults() line 133 sets idTokenSignatureAlgorithm=SignatureAlgorithm.RS256. This is the configuration layer; JwtGenerator line 99 is the runtime layer. Both are valid separate findings. Accepting as-is.

adjudication_records.adjudicator_notes: Human review: RSA-2048 X.509 cert generation via BouncyCastle confirmed from method_body. SHA256WithRSAEncryption = PKCS1v15 padding. Minor edit: security_concern should flag PKCS1v15 padding (same as HAR-002). SHA256WithRSAandMGF1 (RSA-PSS) would be preferred for new X.509 cert generation.

label_corrections.labels.security_concern_note: SHA256WithRSAEncryption uses PKCS1v15 padding; SHA256WithRSAandMGF1 (RSA-PSS) preferred

adjudication_records.human_reasoning: BouncyCastle X509v3CertificateBuilder with SHA256WithRSAEncryption is standard practice but PKCS1v15 padding is legacy. For new certificate generation in 2024+, SHA256WithRSAandMGF1 (RSA-PSS) provides better security properties. Not a critical finding but actionable for algorithm agility.

adjudication_records.adjudicator_notes: Human review: EC P-256 and RSA-2048 key pair generation confirmed for multitenancy. Having both EC and RSA keys is good algorithm agility practice. quantum_vulnerable_public_key correct for both EC (ECDLP) and RSA (IFP). No active security weakness. ECDSA P-256 is NIST-recommended and currently secure. Accepted.
adjudication_records.human_reasoning: EC P-256 key generation from KeyPairGenerator.getInstance('EC') with ECGenParameterSpec('secp256r1'). P-256 is currently the most widely supported NIST curve. The multitenancy pattern of supporting both RSA and EC is a positive finding — demonstrates algorithm agility awareness in the codebase.

adjudication_records.adjudicator_notes: Human review: nimbus-jose-jwt 10.4.2 confirmed from libs.versions.toml. Current release, no known CVEs at time of review. PQC limitation (no ML-KEM/ML-DSA in 10.x) correctly noted. quantum_vulnerable because the library processes RSA/EC keys. No active vulnerability — migration path planning required for PQC roadmap. Accepted.
adjudication_records.human_reasoning: nimbus-jose-jwt is the JOSE implementation backing Spring Security OAuth2. Version 10.4.2 is stable. The library's RSA/EC key handling makes it quantum-relevant at the dependency level. No immediate action needed but should appear in PQC migration roadmap as upgrade target (11.x for ML-DSA support).

adjudication_records.adjudicator_notes: Human review: OAuth2AuthorizationServerConfigurer.configure() wires JWKSource into NimbusJwtEncoder confirmed from method_body. This is the architectural entry point for the JWT signing chain. Outcome correctly identifies this as a crypto_finding at the configuration wiring layer. No independent algorithm selection here — algorithm flows from TokenSettings/JwtGenerator (HAR-002, HAR-007). Accepted.
adjudication_records.human_reasoning: The configurer is architecturally important for CBOM — it shows where the crypto components are assembled. JWKSource (with RSA/EC keys) + NimbusJwtEncoder = the complete JWT signing infrastructure. Correct to capture this as a finding. The quantum vulnerability flows through from the key types in JWKSource.
